docs.title: Homelab Infrastructure as Code
docs.text: A complete infrastructure-as-code solution for managing a homelab running on Proxmox VMs. It automates the entire lifecycle from VM template creation to service deployment.

docs.title: What is this project?
docs.text:
This repository provisions and configures a full homelab environment using modern DevOps tooling:

  • VM Template Creation with Packer
  • Infrastructure Provisioning with Terraform
  • Configuration Management with Ansible
  • Service Deployment with Docker Compose

Everything is version-controlled, encrypted with SOPS, and supports multiple environments.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: An overview of how the homelab infrastructure layers connect.

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text:
The deployment follows a four-stage pipeline:

graph LR
    A[Packer] -->|VM templates| B[Terraform]
    B -->|Provisioned VMs| C[Ansible]
    C -->|Configured hosts| D[Docker]
  1. Packer builds base VM templates (Ubuntu/Debian) on Proxmox with cloud-init
  2. Terraform provisions VMs from those templates across environments
  3. Ansible configures the VMs, installs Docker, and deploys services
  4. Docker Compose runs all application containers

docs.title: Environments
docs.text: Environment Purpose Location WIL Development and primary Local NYC Production Remote

docs.title: Key Design Decisions
docs.text:
• Docker Compose over Kubernetes for simplicity
  • Split-horizon DNS with BIND9 for internal resolution
  • SOPS + Age for secrets encryption at rest
  • Caddy for automatic HTTPS with minimal configuration
  • Task runner for unified command interface

docs.title: Multi-Environment
docs.text: How the homelab supports multiple deployment environments.

docs.title: Environments
docs.text: Name Purpose Description WIL Development / Primary Main homelab environment NYC Production Remote production deployment External VPS External monitoring (Uptime Kuma)

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
Each environment has its own configuration under both Ansible and Terraform:

ansible/environments/
├── wil/
│   ├── hosts.ini
│   └── group_vars/
└── external/
    ├── hosts.ini
    └── group_vars/

terraform/environments/
├── wil/
└── nyc/

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
• Ansible uses inventory files (hosts.ini) and group variables per environment
  • Terraform uses separate terraform.tfvars per environment
  • Packer uses environment-specific variable files
  • Shared configuration lives in group_vars/all/

docs.title: Network Layout
docs.text:
The homelab runs a unified networking stack across multiple sites: internal DNS, a reverse proxy with automatic TLS, dynamic DNS for external access, and a VPN mesh connecting everything together.

Tip

For detailed configuration and howto guides, see the Networking infrastructure docs.

docs.title: Domains
docs.text: Domain Environment Purpose 5am.video WIL (primary) Media services (Plex, *arr stack) 5am.cloud WIL (primary) Top-level cloud services wil.5am.cloud WIL (primary) WIL internal infrastructure (monitoring, apps) ext.5am.cloud WIL (primary) External-facing services sfc.al WIL (primary) Personal projects ldn.5am.cloud LDN (primary) LDN internal infrastructure

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
graph TD
    subgraph External
        ExtClient[External Client]
        Cloudflare[Cloudflare DNS]
        DDNS[DDNS Updater]
    end

    subgraph Internal
        IntClient[Internal Client]
        BIND9[BIND9 DNS]
        Caddy[Caddy Reverse Proxy]
        Backend[Backend Service]
    end

    IntClient -->|DNS query| BIND9
    BIND9 -->|"proxied service"| Caddy
    BIND9 -->|"direct service"| Backend
    Caddy -->|reverse proxy| Backend

    ExtClient -->|DNS query| Cloudflare
    Cloudflare -->|public IP| Caddy
    DDNS -->|updates IP| Cloudflare
  • Internal clients query BIND9 for service hostnames. Proxied services resolve to Caddy, which terminates TLS and forwards to backends. Non-proxied services resolve directly to the backend.
  • External clients resolve via Cloudflare DNS and reach Caddy over the public IP, kept current by DDNS.
  • Cross-site communication runs over Tailscale WireGuard tunnels. DNS zone transfers replicate zones between sites so each site can resolve the other's services.

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• VLANs — each site uses a UDM Pro gateway with VLANs for network segmentation (infrastructure, VMs, storage, clients, IoT). See Gateway (UniFi).
  • Split-Horizon DNS — internal and external clients get different answers for the same hostname. See DNS Services.
  • Unified Service Definitions — a single YAML entry per service drives both DNS records and reverse proxy configuration. See Service Definition Reference.
  • Zone Transfers — WIL and LDN replicate each other's DNS zones for cross-site resolution. See Cross-Site Zone Transfers.
  • Wildcard TLS — Caddy obtains one wildcard certificate per domain via Cloudflare DNS-01 challenge. See TLS Certificate Management.
  • Static Routes — UDM Pro routes cross-site traffic to the Tailscale subnet router VM. See Static Routes.
  • Port Forwarding — UDM Pro forwards ports 80/443 to Caddy for external access. See Port Forwarding.

docs.title: Secrets Management
docs.text: How sensitive configuration is encrypted and managed.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The homelab uses SOPS with Age encryption to store secrets alongside code. Encrypted files are committed to Git and decrypted at deploy time by Ansible.

docs.title: Encrypted File Patterns
docs.text: Pattern Purpose **/secrets.sops.yml Ansible group variable secrets terraform/**/*.tfvars Terraform variable files packer/**/*.pkrvars.hcl Packer variable files docker/**/.env Docker environment files

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Edit secrets with sops <file> (decrypts in-editor, re-encrypts on save)
  2. Commit the encrypted file to Git
  3. Ansible decrypts at deploy time using the community.sops collection

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Before deploying the homelab infrastructure, ensure you have the following prerequisites in place.

docs.title: Proxmox Host(s)
docs.text:
• Minimum: 1 Proxmox server
  • Recommended: 3+ Proxmox servers for HA
  • CPU: 8+ cores (16+ recommended)
  • RAM: 32GB minimum (64GB+ recommended)
  • Storage: 500GB+ SSD/NVMe
  • Network: 1Gbps+ network connection

docs.title: Resource Allocation
docs.text:
For a minimal dev environment, you'll need:

Component VMs CPU per VM RAM per VM Storage per VM K3s Masters 3 2 cores 4GB 50GB K3s Workers 2 2 cores 4GB 50GB Total 5 10 cores 20GB 250GB

docs.title: On Your Local Machine
docs.text:
1. Docker & Docker Compose 

    # Check if installed
docker --version
docker-compose --version

  2. Git 

    # Check if installed
git --version

  3. SSH Client 

    # Generate SSH key if needed
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your-email@example.com"

docs.text:

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: This guide will help you deploy the homelab infrastructure from scratch. Follow these steps in order to set up your complete Kubernetes cluster.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The deployment process consists of 6 main steps:

  1. Prerequisites - Set up your environment
  2. Build Execution Environment - Create the tools container
  3. Create VM Templates - Build base images with Packer
  4. Provision VMs - Deploy infrastructure with Terraform
  5. Deploy K3s - Install Kubernetes with Ansible
  6. Bootstrap GitOps - Set up Flux for application management

docs.title: Step 0: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure you have:

  • A Proxmox server (or cluster) running
  • Docker and Docker Compose installed locally
  • Git installed
  • SSH key pair generated
  • At least 16GB RAM and 200GB storage available

docs.title: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/sfcal/homelab.git
cd homelab

docs.title: Generate SSH Keys (if needed)
docs.text: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your-email@example.com"

docs.title: Step 1: Build Execution Environment
docs.text:
Create a containerized environment with all necessary tools:

# Build the container
cd docker/exe
docker build -t homelab-exe .

# Create an alias for convenience
alias homelab='docker run -it --rm \
  -v "$HOME/.ssh:/home/devops/.ssh" \
  -v "$HOME/.kube:/home/devops/.kube" \
  -v "$PWD:/workspace" \
  -e ENV=dev \
  homelab-exe'

# Test the environment
homelab terraform version
homelab ansible --version

docs.title: Step 2: Create VM Templates with Packer
Configure Packer Variables
docs.text:
cd packer/environments/dev
cp credentials.prod.pkrvars.hcl.example credentials.dev.pkrvars.hcl

# Edit with your Proxmox details
vim credentials.dev.pkrvars.hcl

Example configuration: 

proxmox_api_url = "https://your-proxmox:8006/api2/json"
proxmox_api_token_id = "root@pam!packer"
proxmox_api_token_secret = "your-secret-token"
ssh_password = "temporary-password"

docs.title: Build the Template
docs.text:
cd packer
make build TEMPLATE=base ENV=dev

docs.title: Configure Terraform Variables
docs.text:
cd terraform/environments/dev
cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars

# Edit with your configuration
vim terraform.tfvars

docs.title: Deploy Infrastructure
docs.text:
make deploy ENV=dev

This creates: - 3 Master nodes: 10.1.20.51-53 - 2 Worker nodes: 10.1.20.41-42

docs.title: Configure Ansible
docs.text:
Review the inventory and configuration:

cd ansible
cat environments/dev/hosts.ini
vim environments/dev/group_vars/all.yml

Key settings to verify: - apiserver_endpoint: Virtual IP for the cluster - k3s_token: Change from default! - metal_lb_ip_range: LoadBalancer IP range

docs.title: Deploy K3s
docs.text: make deploy-k3s ENV=dev

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
# Copy kubeconfig
cp kubeconfig ~/.kube/config

# Check cluster
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods -A

docs.title: Install Flux CLI
docs.text: curl -s https://fluxcd.io/install.sh | sudo bash

docs.title: Bootstrap Flux
docs.text:
flux bootstrap github \
  --owner=sfcal \
  --repository=homelab \
  --branch=main \
  --path=./kubernetes/cluster/dev \
  --personal

docs.title: Monitor Deployment
docs.text:
# Watch Flux sync
flux get kustomizations

# Check core services
kubectl get pods -n traefik
kubectl get pods -n cert-manager
kubectl get pods -n longhorn-system

docs.title: Step 6: Access Services
docs.text:
Once deployed, access your services:

  • Traefik: https://traefik.local.samuelcalvert.com
  • Grafana: https://grafana.local.samuelcalvert.com
  • Longhorn: https://longhorn.local.samuelcalvert.com

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Deploy applications
  • Configure monitoring
  • Set up backups

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
1. Packer build fails: Check Proxmox API credentials and network connectivity
  2. Terraform timeout: Ensure Proxmox has sufficient resources
  3. K3s won't start: Verify network configuration and firewall rules
  4. Flux sync errors: Check GitHub token permissions

For more help, see the FAQ or open an issue on GitHub.

docs.title: Infrastructure
docs.text: Reference documentation for each layer of the infrastructure pipeline.

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text:
• Packer — VM template creation for Proxmox
  • Terraform — VM provisioning and lifecycle management
  • Ansible — Configuration management and service deployment
  • Docker Services — Container definitions and compose templates

docs.title: Infrastructure Services
docs.text:
Deployed by Ansible in dependency order:

  1. Networking — DNS (BIND9), reverse proxy (Caddy), dynamic DNS, VPN (Tailscale)
  2. Certificate Authority — Private CA (Step-CA) for internal TLS certificates
  3. Time Server — NTP synchronization (Chrony)
  4. Monitoring — Metrics (Prometheus), dashboards (Grafana, Homepage), uptime (Uptime Kuma)

docs.title: Ansible
docs.text: Configuration management and service deployment using Ansible.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Ansible configures provisioned VMs and deploys all services. A master playbook (site.yml) orchestrates the full deployment in dependency order.

docs.title: Infrastructure
docs.text: Playbook Purpose Docs infrastructure/networking DNS (BIND9), reverse proxy (Caddy), DDNS, Tailscale Networking infrastructure/ca Private certificate authority (Step-CA) Certificate Authority infrastructure/ntp NTP time server (Chrony) Time Server infrastructure/monitoring Prometheus, Grafana, Homepage Monitoring infrastructure/external-monitoring Uptime Kuma on external VPS Monitoring

docs.title: Applications
docs.text: Playbook Purpose apps/media Plex + *arr media stack apps/games-server Terraria server apps/website Personal website apps/birdle Birdle game

docs.title: Roles
docs.text: Role Purpose common Base system configuration (timezone, apt cache) docker_service Generic Docker Compose service deployment tailscale Tailscale VPN client/subnet router setup

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
task ansible:deploy-all              # Deploy everything
task ansible:deploy-networking       # Networking stack
task ansible:deploy-ca               # Certificate authority
task ansible:deploy-ntp              # NTP time server
task ansible:deploy-monitoring       # Monitoring stack
task ansible:deploy-external-monitoring  # External uptime monitoring
task ansible:deploy-media            # Media stack
task ansible:ping                    # Test connectivity

docs.title: Docker Services
docs.text: All homelab services run as Docker Compose stacks deployed by Ansible.

docs.title: Media Stack
docs.text:
The media stack provides automated media management and streaming:

Service Purpose Plex Media streaming server Sonarr TV show management Radarr Movie management Prowlarr Indexer management Sabnzbd Download client Bazarr Subtitle management Tdarr Media transcoding Pulsarr Request management Frigate Camera/NVR integration

docs.title: Infrastructure Services
docs.text: Service Purpose Caddy Reverse proxy with auto-HTTPS BIND9 Internal DNS server Prometheus Metrics collection Grafana Metrics visualization Homepage Dashboard Tailscale VPN/subnet routing

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Ansible uses Jinja2 templates to generate docker-compose.yml files from group variables, then deploys them using the docker_service role. This allows environment-specific configuration while keeping the compose structure consistent.

docs.title: Packer
docs.text: VM template creation for Proxmox using HashiCorp Packer.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Packer builds base VM images that Terraform uses to provision VMs. Templates include cloud-init for automated first-boot configuration.

docs.title: Templates
docs.text: Template Base OS Purpose ubuntu-server-base Ubuntu Server Primary VM template debian-bookworm-base Debian 12 Alternative base template

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Templates are defined in HCL files under packer/templates/ with environment-specific variables in packer/environments/.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: task packer:build

docs.title: Terraform
docs.text: VM provisioning on Proxmox using HashiCorp Terraform.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Terraform provisions VMs from Packer-built templates. VMs are defined as a map in terraform.tfvars, making it easy to add or remove machines.

docs.title: Module
docs.text:
The modules/vm/ module handles generic VM provisioning with configurable:

  • CPU, memory, and disk resources
  • Network configuration
  • Cloud-init settings
  • SSH key injection

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
task terraform:deploy    # Apply infrastructure changes
task terraform:destroy   # Tear down infrastructure
task terraform:clean     # Clean up state

docs.title: File Structure
docs.text:
terraform/
├── main.tf
├── variables.tf
├── outputs.tf
├── providers.tf
├── versions.tf
├── modules/vm/
└── environments/
    ├── wil/
    └── nyc/

docs.title: Certificate Authority (Step-CA)
docs.text:
Step-CA provides a private certificate authority for issuing internal TLS certificates. It runs as a Docker container on a dedicated VM in each environment, enabling services to communicate over HTTPS without relying on public CAs.

Deployment order

Step-CA deploys after Networking and before NTP, monitoring, and applications.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    StepCA[Step-CA Server]
    Service1[Service A]
    Service2[Service B]
    RootCert[Root CA Certificate]

    StepCA -->|issues cert| Service1
    StepCA -->|issues cert| Service2
    StepCA -->|exports| RootCert
    RootCert -->|trusted by| Service1
    RootCert -->|trusted by| Service2

Step-CA initializes on first run with a root CA certificate and an admin provisioner. Services request certificates via the ACME protocol or the step CLI. The root CA certificate is exported and can be distributed to clients for trust.

docs.title: File Locations
docs.text: File Purpose playbooks/infrastructure/ca/deploy.yml Main playbook playbooks/infrastructure/ca/tasks/step-ca.yml Installation and configuration task playbooks/infrastructure/ca/templates/compose.yaml.j2 Docker Compose service definition playbooks/infrastructure/ca/handlers/main.yml Container lifecycle handlers environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_ca/vars.yml Per-environment CA variables environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_ca/secrets.sops.yml Encrypted provisioner password

docs.title: Hosts
docs.text: Environment IP FQDN WIL 10.2.20.9 ca.wil.5am.cloud LDN 10.3.20.9 ca.ldn.5am.cloud

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
task ansible:deploy-ca ENV=wil

The task file performs these steps in order:

  1. Creates directory structure at /opt/step-ca/ and /opt/step-ca/data/
  2. Deploys the provisioner password file (mode 0600)
  3. Deploys compose.yaml from template
  4. Starts the Step-CA container
  5. Waits 10 seconds for initialization
  6. Runs a health check against https://127.0.0.1:9000/health
  7. Configures certificate duration claims in ca.json via jq
  8. Exports the root CA certificate to /opt/step-ca/root_ca.crt
  9. Displays status output

Source: ansible/playbooks/infrastructure/ca/tasks/step-ca.yml

docs.title: Docker Compose
docs.text:
The container runs smallstep/step-ca with the following configuration:

  • Port: 9000 (HTTPS API)
  • Volumes: ./data for CA state, ./password.txt mounted read-only
  • Restart policy: unless-stopped
  • Initialization: handled via DOCKER_STEPCA_INIT_* environment variables on first run

Source: ansible/playbooks/infrastructure/ca/templates/compose.yaml.j2

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: All variables are set in ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_ca/vars.yml.

docs.title: step_ca_version
docs.text:
Docker image tag for the Step-CA container.

Type: string

WILLDN 
step_ca_version: "0.27.5"

step_ca_version: "0.27.5"

docs.title: step_ca_port
docs.text:
Port the Step-CA HTTPS API listens on.

Type: integer

Default: 9000

step_ca_port: 9000

docs.title: step_ca_name
docs.text:
Display name for the certificate authority. Used during initialization and appears in issued certificates.

Type: string

WILLDN 
step_ca_name: "WIL Homelab CA"

step_ca_name: "LDN Homelab CA"

docs.title: step_ca_dns_names
docs.text:
Comma-separated list of DNS names and IPs the CA server responds to. Used to generate the CA's own TLS certificate.

Type: string

WILLDN 
step_ca_dns_names: "ca.wil.5am.cloud,10.2.20.9,127.0.0.1"

step_ca_dns_names: "ca.ldn.5am.cloud,10.3.0.9,127.0.0.1"

docs.title: step_ca_provisioner_name
docs.text:
Name of the admin provisioner used to issue certificates.

Type: string

Default: "admin"

step_ca_provisioner_name: "admin"

docs.title: step_ca_init_ssh
docs.text:
Whether to initialize SSH certificate support.

Type: string

Default: "false"

step_ca_init_ssh: "false"

docs.title: step_ca_uid / step_ca_gid
docs.text:
UID and GID for the container's file ownership.

Type: string

Default: "1000"

step_ca_uid: "1000"
step_ca_gid: "1000"

docs.title: step_ca_default_cert_duration
docs.text:
Default certificate validity period. Applied to ca.json claims after initialization.

Type: string

Default: "720h" (30 days)

step_ca_default_cert_duration: "720h"

docs.title: step_ca_max_cert_duration
docs.text:
Maximum allowed certificate validity period.

Type: string

Default: "17520h" (2 years)

step_ca_max_cert_duration: "17520h"

docs.title: step_ca_provisioner_password
docs.text:
Password for the admin provisioner. Used to authenticate certificate requests.

Type: string

Warning

Stored in SOPS-encrypted secrets.sops.yml, never in plaintext.

Sources: ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_ca/vars.yml · ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_ca/secrets.sops.yml

docs.title: Verify CA health
docs.text:
task ca:health ENV=wil

Or manually:

curl -k https://ca.wil.5am.cloud:9000/health

docs.title: Export the root CA certificate
docs.text:
The root certificate is automatically exported during deployment to /opt/step-ca/root_ca.crt. To retrieve it:

ssh sfcal@10.2.20.9 cat /opt/step-ca/root_ca.crt

docs.title: Change certificate duration
docs.text:
1. Edit ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_ca/vars.yml:

    step_ca_default_cert_duration: "2160h"   # 90 days
step_ca_max_cert_duration: "17520h"      # 2 years

  2. Deploy:

    task ansible:deploy-ca ENV=wil

The task applies duration changes to ca.json via jq on each run.

docs.title: Restart the CA
docs.text:
The playbook handlers support lifecycle management:

# Full redeploy (will restart automatically if config changes)
task ansible:deploy-ca ENV=wil

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
The monitoring stack provides metrics collection, visualization, a service dashboard, and external uptime monitoring. It runs as Docker containers on a dedicated VM in each environment.

Deployment order

Monitoring deploys after Networking, Step-CA, and NTP. It is the last infrastructure service to deploy before applications.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    subgraph Monitoring VM
        Prometheus[Prometheus]
        Grafana[Grafana]
        Homepage[Homepage]
    end

    subgraph Targets
        NE1[Node Exporter\nmonitoring VM]
        NE2[Node Exporter\nmedia-stack]
        NE3[Node Exporter\nnetworking]
        NE4[Node Exporter\nwebsite]
    end

    subgraph External VPS
        UptimeKuma[Uptime Kuma]
    end

    NE1 & NE2 & NE3 & NE4 -->|metrics| Prometheus
    Prometheus -->|datasource| Grafana
    UptimeKuma -->|health checks| Prometheus
  • Prometheus scrapes node exporters across all VMs for system metrics
  • Grafana visualizes metrics from Prometheus with dashboards
  • Homepage provides a service dashboard with status widgets and quick links
  • Uptime Kuma runs on an external VPS for independent uptime monitoring via Tailscale

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
# Deploy internal monitoring stack
task ansible:deploy-monitoring ENV=wil

# Deploy external uptime monitoring
task ansible:deploy-external-monitoring ENV=external

docs.title: Monitoring Stack Deployment
docs.text:
The task file:

  1. Creates directory structure under /opt/monitoring/
  2. Sets ownership to monitoring_uid:monitoring_gid
  3. Deploys Prometheus configuration
  4. Deploys Grafana datasource provisioning
  5. Deploys Homepage configuration files (services, bookmarks, settings, widgets)
  6. Deploys Docker Compose file
  7. Starts all containers

docs.title: External Monitoring Deployment
docs.text:
The external monitoring playbook:

  1. Runs the common role (timezone, apt cache)
  2. Installs and configures Tailscale as a client (to reach internal services)
  3. Deploys Uptime Kuma via the docker_service role

docs.title: Prometheus
docs.text: Prometheus scrapes metrics from node exporters running on infrastructure and application VMs.

docs.title: Scrape Configuration
docs.text:
The prometheus.yml.j2 template generates the scrape config:

global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
  evaluation_interval: 15s

scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'prometheus'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['localhost:9090']

  - job_name: 'node-exporter'
    static_configs:
      - targets:
          - 'localhost:9100'
          - '10.2.0.5:9100'
          # ... all node targets

docs.title: Container Configuration
docs.text:
• Memory: 2GB limit, 1GB reservation
  • Retention: configurable via prometheus_retention
  • Storage: persistent volume at /prometheus
  • User: runs as monitoring_uid:monitoring_gid

Source: ansible/playbooks/infrastructure/monitoring/templates/prometheus.yml.j2

docs.text:
Docker image tag for Prometheus.

Type: string

prometheus_version: "v3.3.0"

docs.title: prometheus_retention
docs.text:
How long to retain metrics data.

Type: string

Default: "30d"

prometheus_retention: "30d"

docs.title: prometheus_node_targets
docs.text:
List of node exporter endpoints to scrape. Each entry is a host:port string.

Type: list[string]

WILLDN 
prometheus_node_targets:
  - "localhost:9100"
  - "10.2.0.5:9100"
  - "10.2.20.53:9100"
  - "10.2.20.45:9100"

prometheus_node_targets:
  - "localhost:9100"
  - "10.3.0.53:9100"

Sources: ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_monitoring/prometheus.yml · ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_monitoring/containers.yml

docs.title: Grafana
docs.text: Grafana connects to Prometheus as its default datasource and provides metric visualization dashboards.

docs.title: Datasource Provisioning
docs.text:
Grafana auto-provisions a Prometheus datasource on startup via grafana-datasources.yml.j2:

apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Prometheus
    type: prometheus
    access: proxy
    url: http://prometheus:9090
    isDefault: true
    editable: true

docs.title: Container Configuration
docs.text:
• Memory: 2GB limit, 1GB reservation
  • Depends on: Prometheus
  • Provisioning: mounted read-only at /etc/grafana/provisioning
  • Sign-up: disabled (GF_USERS_ALLOW_SIGN_UP=false)

docs.title: Homepage
docs.text: Homepage provides a service dashboard with status widgets, service health indicators, and quick links to all homelab services.

docs.title: Container Configuration
docs.text:
• Memory: 512MB limit, 256MB reservation
  • Docker socket: mounted read-only for container status widgets
  • Environment: receives API keys for service widgets (Sonarr, Radarr, Bazarr, Prowlarr, SABnzbd, Plex)

docs.title: homepage_version
docs.text:
Docker image tag for Homepage.

Type: string

Default: "latest"

homepage_version: "latest"

docs.title: homepage_allowed_hosts
docs.text:
Comma-separated list of hostnames Homepage will respond to. Required for security.

Type: string

WILLDN 
homepage_allowed_hosts: "homepage.wil.5am.cloud"

homepage_allowed_hosts: "homepage.ldn.5am.cloud"

docs.title: Dashboard Configuration
docs.text:
Homepage is configured via four YAML template files:

Template Purpose services.yaml.j2 Service cards with status widgets (media, monitoring, node exporters) bookmarks.yaml.j2 Quick links (TrueNAS, Proxmox) settings.yaml.j2 Theme (dark, slate), layout (row style, column counts) widgets.yaml.j2 Global widgets (search bar, datetime)

Sources: ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_monitoring/containers.yml · ansible/playbooks/infrastructure/monitoring/templates/services.yaml.j2

docs.title: External Monitoring (Uptime Kuma)
docs.text: Uptime Kuma runs on an external VPS to provide independent uptime monitoring. It connects to the internal network via Tailscale to monitor services that are not publicly exposed.

docs.title: uptime_kuma_version
docs.text:
Docker image tag for Uptime Kuma.

Type: string

uptime_kuma_version: "2"

docs.title: external_monitoring_uptime_kuma_listen
docs.text:
Port Uptime Kuma listens on.

Type: string

Default: "3001"

external_monitoring_uptime_kuma_listen: "3001"

docs.title: Tailscale Integration
docs.text:
The external VPS runs Tailscale as a client with route acceptance enabled:

tailscale_mode: "client"
tailscale_hostname: "external-monitor"
tailscale_accept_routes: true

This allows Uptime Kuma to reach internal services (e.g., 10.2.20.53) through the Tailscale mesh without exposing them publicly. See VPN (Tailscale) for details.

Sources: ansible/environments/external/group_vars/infra_externalmonitoring/vars.yml · ansible/playbooks/infrastructure/external-monitoring/templates/compose.yaml.j2
